[LOW] citation: no CITATION.cff — scientific users can't mechanically cite frankenpandas in papers
description:
Academic-citation metadata audit.

**Observation.** `ls CITATION.cff` → No such file. No README section on "how to cite this in a paper." No DOI / Zenodo integration.

**Why this matters for a pandas-parity library.** pandas itself is cited in ~50k academic papers per year — it's the canonical data-analysis tool in scientific Python. frankenpandas positions itself as "clean-room Rust reimplementation of the full pandas API surface" targeting the same user base. Scientific users WILL want to cite the library in their methods sections once adoption grows.

**What CITATION.cff is.** YAML-format standard for software citation metadata. GitHub auto-detects the file and renders a "Cite this repository" button + generates BibTeX / APA / etc. on demand.

**Severity.** LOW — no functional impact, no release blocker, no swarm-velocity friction. But: if we ship 0.1.0 to crates.io without a citation format, the first academic user to adopt the library opens an issue asking "how do I cite this?" — easy to preempt.

**Fix shape.** `CITATION.cff` at repo root:
```yaml
cff-version: 1.2.0
message: "If you use frankenpandas in published work, please cite it."
title: "frankenpandas: clean-room Rust reimplementation of the pandas API"
authors:
  - family-names: "Doodle"
    given-names: "Jeffrey E"    # or whichever upstream maintainer names
    orcid: ""                    # optional
repository-code: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
url: "https://frankenpandas.dev"
license: MIT
version: "0.1.0"
date-released: "2026-XX-XX"       # fill in at release
keywords:
  - dataframe
  - pandas
  - rust
  - data-analysis
  - columnar-storage
preferred-citation:
  type: software
  authors: <as above>
  title: "frankenpandas"
  url: "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
```

Once GitHub renders the "Cite this repository" button, academic users get BibTeX they can paste into papers. Zero ongoing maintenance beyond version bumps at release.

Optional companion: Zenodo integration. Push a release tag → Zenodo mints a DOI → CITATION.cff updates with the DOI → papers can cite the specific version. Free; takes ~5 min of setup once.

Blast radius: 1 new file (~20 lines). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow) — CITATION.cff's version field updates per release; consider automation.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — "How do I cite this?" section links to CITATION.cff.
- README capability table — "Cite this repository" GitHub button is a free marketing surface.

title: fp-conformance: excel_round_trip emits index column by default but reads without index_col
description:
Observed: tests::packet_filter_runs_io_round_trip_packet — 1/10 case fails: excel_round_trip_basic_strict returns actual=false expected=true.

Root: execute_excel_round_trip_fixture_operation (crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:12074) writes via write_excel_bytes (default ExcelWriteOptions{ index: true, ... }, matching pandas) but reads via read_excel_bytes(default ExcelReadOptions, no index_col). Round-trip therefore returns a frame with one extra leading column (the original index), so column_names() diverges and dataframes_semantically_equal returns false.

Fix: write with ExcelWriteOptions{ index: false, ..default }, OR write with index AND read with matching index_col. Simpler & equally valid path: use ExcelWriteOptions { index: false, ..default } in the round-trip helper. Restores parity for excel_round_trip_basic_strict; doesn't touch the public default which must keep index=true to match pandas' to_excel default.
close_reason: shipped 6468730: switched execute_excel_round_trip_fixture_operation to write_excel_bytes_with_options(index=false) so writer output matches default reader input. packet_filter_runs_io_round_trip_packet now passes 10/10. Public write_excel_bytes default (index=true, pandas-aligned) unchanged. Clippy clean.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch target (HashMap+Vec aggfunc routing)
description:
fuzz/fuzz_targets/ has fuzz_groupby_sum but no coverage for the variadic agg dispatch surface that just landed multiple beads (br-715r dict-of-list, br-no1t transform_fn callable, etc.). The match-arm dispatch in DataFrameGroupBy::agg/agg_list/agg_dict_list/agg_named is wide enough to harbor unhandled-variant panics that cargo test won't surface (e.g. unrecognized aggfunc names crash with FrameError but a panic in Series::skew/kurt with degenerate inputs would cascade).

Proposed target: fuzz_groupby_agg.rs with:
1. Synth mode: 1 group key col (Utf8) + 1 numeric col, 2-16 rows, group cardinality picked from input bytes.
2. Pick aggfunc set from a fixed enum: a single string ('sum'/'mean'/etc), a Vec<String> for agg_list, a HashMap<String, String> for agg, or a HashMap<String, Vec<String>> for agg_dict_list.
3. Run the corresponding agg method; if Ok, assert output rows == unique groups × outputs-per-input-col.
4. If Err, assert it is FrameError::CompatibilityRejected (no panic).

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes(input: &[u8]). Catches regressions like skew on len<3 groups where the underlying Series::skew can return NaN but the dispatch must propagate it cleanly.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: verified parallel agent's commit 3ee01b2 implements the bead spec. fp-conformance adds fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes (lib.rs:6895) which projects bytes into a groupby-ready frame (grp + val cols) plus a func list via fuzz_groupby_agg_funcs_from_bytes, then exercises all four agg-dispatch entry points via a mode_tag % 4 switch — agg_list(&Vec<&str>), agg(HashMap<col, func>), agg_dict_list(HashMap<col, Vec<func>>), and agg_named(Vec<(output, col, func)>). Goes beyond the bead's proposed HashMap + single agg routing. Invariant check is fuzz_validate_groupby_agg_result which asserts group count and column count match expectation. fuzz/Cargo.toml has the matching [[bin]] fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch block; fuzz_targets/fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch.rs wraps fp_conformance::fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes under libfuzzer_sys. Tests: 4/4 unit tests pass under rch (fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes_accepts_empty_input + ..._compat_rejected_seed + ..._supported_agg_list_seed + ..._named_aggregation_seed).

title: [LOW] fp-conformance assert_excel_roundtrip helper diverges from public default to satisfy reader; document as test-only gap
description:
Reviewing 989b/mxho fixes (cc): both excel round-trip helpers were patched to write with index=false because the default reader doesn't promote the leading index column back. Public write_excel_bytes default remains pandas-aligned (index=true). This is a test-only divergence — the harness now exercises a non-default code path.

Risk: production users calling write_excel_bytes(default) and read_excel_bytes(default) will hit the same column-leak bug the harness sidesteps. The conformance suite no longer surfaces this regression because the harness avoids it.

Suggested follow-up:
1. Add a separate fixture (or augment FP-P2D-129/excel_round_trip_basic_strict) that writes with index=true and reads with the matching index_col so the default-pair round-trip is genuinely tested end-to-end.
2. Or: ship a read_excel_bytes default that auto-detects the leading index column emitted by write_excel_bytes (via a sentinel header or 'inferred index_col' option) so the defaults round-trip cleanly.

Low priority: doesn't block conformance-to-zero work, but the test harness is currently 'green for the wrong reason' on this surface.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: the inline comment at crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:5020-5025 already documents the helper's divergence (index=false) from the public write_excel_bytes default (index=true), citing br-989b. Both suggested follow-ups (fixture with matching index_col, or reader-side index auto-detection) are product-level scope decisions rather than conformance-gate bugs and do not block conformance-to-zero. Deferring the follow-up as a documented known issue; reopen if the default write/read pair is reprioritized.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no memory/time bounds on fuzz harness — crafted input can OOM-kill or timeout-kill silently
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs" anti-pattern + the related OUTPUT-size gap.

**Observation.** Every existing fuzz target has an INPUT size cap:
  fuzz_csv_parse.rs:        MAX_CSV_BYTES    = 64 KB
  fuzz_fixture_parse.rs:    MAX_FIXTURE_BYTES = 64 KB
  fuzz_feather_io.rs:       MAX_FEATHER_BYTES = 512 KB
  fuzz_excel_io.rs:         MAX_EXCEL_BYTES   = 512 KB
  fuzz_dataframe_eval.rs:   MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES = 256 KB
  etc.

Good — that's the skill's minimum defense (anti-pattern #2 fixed). But there is NO corresponding cap on:
- Peak allocation during parse (the parser can decompress / expand / materialize memory much larger than the input).
- Result DataFrame size (a parser that produces a 1B-row frame from a 64KB input is a logic bug but not a panic).
- Total wall-clock time per invocation (an O(n²) parser on a 64KB input at 10^9 ops = minutes).

**Concrete amplification vectors to worry about:**
- `zip`-compressed Excel/.xlsx payloads — a 512KB xlsx can legitimately decompress to 500 MB of sharedStrings XML. calamine's Excel reader mallocs during decompress.
- Deeply nested JSON in read_json_str — 256 KB of {"a":{"a":{"a":... reaches thousands of levels; serde_json can stack-overflow or allocate per-level frame. Our MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES cap helps with the INPUT dimension but not with the recursion-depth / heap-alloc dimension.
- Parquet rowgroup metadata that claims 2B rows in a single group — parquet reader attempts to preallocate a Vec<Int64> of that size → OOM.
- SQL queries (if fuzzed per sibling br-gpxk) constructing huge result sets via cross-join.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Anti-pattern #2: "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs." Partially addressed (input guard exists) but the skill's intent is that we should also have a memory-limit / alloc-hook so OOM doesn't kill the fuzzer silently.
- Rule 1: 1000 exec/s minimum for parsers, 100 for stateful. Long-running crash inputs (not-quite-hang, not-quite-crash) violate this by slowing exec/s without emitting a crash. Time-limit the harness.

**Severity.** HIGH — we don't detect memory-DoS or time-DoS via crafted input. Real users running our library against untrusted data (a common pattern: an analytics pipeline accepting customer-uploaded CSVs) could be hit with a denial-of-service that our fuzz corpus can't surface. The README's fp-io positioning as a drop-in pandas reader means users will point it at adversarial inputs.

**Fix shape:**

1. New global wrapper macro / helper applied at the top of every fuzz_target! body:
   ```rust
   const MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN: usize = 16 * 1024 * 1024;   // 16 MB per invocation
   const MAX_WALL_CLOCK: Duration = Duration::from_millis(500);

   fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| {
       let _guard = cap_memory_and_time(MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN, MAX_WALL_CLOCK);
       if data.len() > MAX_EXCEL_BYTES { return; }
       let _ = fp_conformance::fuzz_excel_io_bytes(data);
   });
   ```

2. `cap_memory_and_time` implementation options:
   - libFuzzer-level: `-rss_limit_mb=16 -timeout=1` flags. Simple, no code change beyond fuzz/Cargo.toml per-bin args.
   - Custom global allocator wrapping `std::alloc::System` with a runtime-accumulated byte counter; returns AllocError when over budget. Integrates with the invariant-checker pattern. More intrusive but catches logic bugs, not just DoS.
   - Both. libFuzzer flags for coarse RSS cap + tracking allocator for fine-grained tracking per test case.

3. For CSV/Excel/Parquet targets: add a per-format cap on the reader's internal `with_capacity` calls. Upstream crates (calamine, parquet) already accept bounds via config — route the bound through.

4. Regression test: once the cap is in place, add a "known-bomb" test case (handcrafted file that attempts to OOM the old path). Include as a permanent fuzz seed so the cap is always exercised.

Blast radius: fuzz/Cargo.toml (+~8 lines: rss_limit_mb + timeout per [[bin]]), new helper in fuzz/src/cap.rs (+~30 lines), 5-10 crafted regression-bomb seeds added to corpus. No changes to fp-* source.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — the CI job needs to surface OOM / timeout as distinct failure categories (not silent kills).
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — the differential oracle's python subprocess has its own memory policy; need to align to avoid Rust-succeeds / python-OOMs divergence.
- br-k05t (scale fixtures) — conformance at scale; sibling goal but separate mechanism (conformance tests, not fuzz).

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no concurrency / TSan campaign — DataFrame Send+Sync contracts + parallel iteration safety unverified
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill decision tree: "Concurrency code → TSan campaign".

**Observation.** The existing fuzz corpus runs single-threaded. No target has a `thread::spawn`, no `rayon::par_iter`, no cross-thread DataFrame reference. skill's Sanitizer Discipline table: "TSan if target has concurrency" — we have concurrency latent in the type system but no TSan campaign.

**What's concurrency-relevant in fp-frame despite single-threaded core logic:**

1. **Send + Sync contracts.** DataFrame, Series, Column, Index, MultiIndex all DERIVE Clone (some auto-derive Send/Sync via Vec<Scalar>). Users legitimately pass frames across thread boundaries (`thread::spawn(move || df.clone().sum())`). If any internal field has interior mutability that the compiler auto-disables Send/Sync for — good, compile error. If any `Arc<Mutex<...>>` internal state exists — we need TSan to verify it behaves across parallel iteration. Cargo tree suggests: bumpalo (single-threaded arena), arrow (Send+Sync-assuming), rusqlite (Connection: !Send by default).

2. **Parallel iteration via downstream consumers.** README advertises "Parallel execution (rayon) | Not started; architecture supports it (columns are independent)". But the PUBLIC API already exposes `&Column`, `&Series`, `.values()` — a user writing `rayon::par_iter` over a DataFrame's columns must trust our data types don't share interior-mutable state between columns. Unverified by our test suite.

3. **future: br-fd90 SQL backends.** tokio-postgres is async; `Connection: Send + !Sync` (typical async-client shape). The SqlConnection trait will need careful Send/Sync bounds.

4. **future: br-diic PyO3 bindings.** Python's GIL wraps, but once `py.allow_threads(|| df.sum())` pattern lands (the standard PyO3 escape hatch for compute-heavy calls), Rust-side thread safety becomes load-bearing.

**Skill rule invoked:** Pattern 7 (Concurrency archetype) + Sanitizer Discipline ("TSan campaign for concurrent code"). Current test suite's entire concurrency coverage is `#[test] fn send_sync_traits_auto_derive() {}` — implicit compile-time check, no runtime verification.

**Severity.** HIGH — concurrency bugs are (a) the hardest to reproduce, (b) often silent for months until a hot production path exposes them, (c) a common cause of "seemed fine in tests, corrupted data in prod" disasters. For a dataframe library targeting pandas parity + Rust performance, concurrency safety is part of the implicit contract.

**Fix shape:**

1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_parallel_dataframe.rs` — wraps `fp_conformance::fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes`.

2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Project bytes into a frame via `fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes` (existing helper).
   - Clone the frame. Spawn 4-8 rayon par_iter jobs over clones, each running a different op:
       thread 1: sum_across_columns(&clone)
       thread 2: series.rolling(5).mean()
       thread 3: groupby + count
       thread 4: merge with a second frame
   - Join all threads. Assert each thread's result matches the same op run single-threaded on the original frame.
   - Invariant: arithmetic on &DataFrame must be pure (no shared-state mutation between threads).

3. CI setup for TSan:
   - `RUSTFLAGS="-Zsanitizer=thread" cargo +nightly fuzz run fuzz_parallel_dataframe` — separate build (TSan incompatible with ASan).
   - Optional: add a scheduled nightly CI job for TSan runs (skill's Rule 6: separate sanitizer campaigns).

4. Shadow-model concurrency assertion (Phase 2):
   - Use `loom` for exhaustive concurrency exploration of critical fast paths (e.g. groupby's HashMap-based accumulator if it ever goes parallel).
   - Today this is overkill — but file a follow-up bead once br-fd90 async SQL lands and we have genuine concurrency code to verify.

Seed corpus:
- empty frame + 1 thread
- 1-row frame + 2 threads (likely-safe smoke test)
- 100-row frame + 8 threads (stress the rayon work-stealing path)
- frame with a Categorical column + parallel groupby (categorical metadata must not race)

Expected cost: TSan is 5-15x slowdown per skill's sanitizer table. Fuzz_parallel_dataframe runs at maybe 50-100 exec/s (well below the 1000/s parser floor, but WITHIN the 100/s stateful floor which this effectively is).

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — sanitizer campaigns need their own CI job config (one per sanitizer, shared corpus per skill Rule 6).
- br-gztr (fuzz dictionaries + sanitizers) — this bead is the concurrency slice of gztr's "configure sanitizers" item.
- br-fd90 slice 1/7 (SqlConnection trait) — Send/Sync bounds on the trait need fuzz-verification.
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) — `py.allow_threads(|| ...)` requires Rust-side thread safety; this bead lights up the path.

title: [HIGH] conformance: 1249 golden fixtures have no pandas-version provenance — silent drift against pandas releases
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Fixture Provenance rule + the "fixtures can become stale" concern.

**Observation.** crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ contains 1249 JSON fixture files. Each carries expected_series or expected_frame payloads computed by pandas_oracle.py at some generation point. None of the fixture files record:
- Which pandas version produced them
- When they were generated
- Which oracle script commit generated them

The fixtures/README.md describes the generation workflow but the Reference line says "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)" — no version pin. .github/workflows/ci.yml neither installs pandas nor regenerates fixtures; it compares Rust output against the frozen 2026 snapshot.

**Concrete risk.** Pandas 2.3.0, 3.0.0, etc. will land. When a pandas point release changes a semantic (they do — see https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/ changelog), our 1249 fixtures keep reflecting the OLD behavior. Rust passes against the old behavior and CI stays green, BUT real users calling frankenpandas and pandas side-by-side hit divergence. The longer the fixtures age without regeneration, the wider the drift.

**Severity.** HIGH — this is a time-bomb: conformance-against-past-pandas silently replaces conformance-against-current-pandas. It will only surface when a user reports a divergence that our own test suite declares impossible.

**Fix shape:**
1. Pin pandas (closes br-frankenpandas-8irq): add crates/fp-conformance/oracle/requirements.txt with pandas==X.Y.Z. CI installs exactly this version.
2. Record provenance in every generated fixture: add pandas_version, oracle_script_sha, generated_at to the fixture JSON (one new optional field; serde skip_if_none so old fixtures stay readable).
3. Stale-fixture detector: new scripts/check_fixture_freshness.sh compares fixture pandas_version field to oracle/requirements.txt pin. Fails CI on mismatch.
4. Quarterly regeneration ritual: document in AGENTS.md a "fixture regen" playbook — bump pandas pin → regenerate all fixtures → `git diff fixtures/` → review every diff line (skill's rule) → file DISCREPANCIES.md entries for any intentional divergences → commit atomically. First regen after this bead lands.
5. Decouple live-oracle tests from the fixture corpus: live_oracle_* tests (closed by br-d6xa sibling CRITICAL bead on CI-pandas-install) catch drift as it happens; fixture-expected tests catch architecture regressions. Both layers needed.

Blast radius: scripts/ (+1 new file, ~40 lines), crates/fp-conformance/oracle/ (+1 requirements.txt), AGENTS.md (+1 playbook section), fixture schema (+3 optional fields, backward-compat).

Depends on br-frankenpandas-d6xa landing first (CI needs pandas to install to regenerate fixtures).

[HIGH] fp-frame grouped-window flat-string label is not real MultiIndex (r4au)
description:
Reviewing 2f80719 (br-frankenpandas-r4au, cod): GroupByRolling::apply_grouped_rolling and GroupByResample paths now emit a flattened string label like 'IndexLabel::Utf8("(a, 2024-01)")' as a workaround for missing row MultiIndex support (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25735 grouped_window_label).

Findings:
- pandas-observable parity is broken: pandas returns a real MultiIndex(levels=[group, bucket], codes=[..]) which downstream code can index via .loc[("a", "2024-01")] or stack/unstack. A formatted Utf8 label cannot be re-decomposed and breaks group-key extraction in joins/aggregates.
- Tests under r4au were rewritten to assert against the new string format (assert_utf8_index_labels("(a, 2024-01)")), so the fp-frame unit tests are 'green' against the workaround instead of pandas truth.
- New emit-only-value-column path drops the group-key column from the output, so consumers expecting both grouping cols and value cols (the as_index=False contract) now see different shapes depending on as_index gate.

Suggested follow-up:
1. Land genuine row MultiIndex via fp-index::MultiIndex (or document why it's deferred).
2. Until then, gate the flat-string workaround behind a non-default GroupByOptions feature so default groupby().rolling/resample preserves the previous shape (group_col + bucket-only index) rather than silently changing the output schema.
3. Update the r4au tests to mark the flat-string label as a workaround assertion (constant in test scope), not a parity claim.

title: Add dataframe head/tail saturating negative-n strict conformance packets
[CRITICAL] conformance: 89 live_oracle_* tests silently skip in CI — core pandas-parity claim is not enforced
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 1 (Differential Testing) + the "Before claiming conformance, prove it" rule.

**Observation.** 89 live_oracle_*_matches_pandas tests exist in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs. Each one dispatches through capture_live_oracle_expected → subprocess pandas_oracle.py. The default HarnessConfig sets:

  oracle_root: repo_root.join("legacy_pandas_code/pandas")
  allow_system_pandas_fallback: false

When oracle_root.exists() is false, capture_live_oracle_expected returns HarnessError::OracleUnavailable. The 89 tests match on that variant and early-return with an eprintln!("live pandas unavailable; skipping ..."). No test fails. No test is marked ignored. No summary counter is emitted.

**The repo intentionally gitignores legacy_pandas_code/** (confirmed: .gitignore line "legacy_pandas_code/"). .github/workflows/ci.yml does NOT install pandas, does NOT set FP_PYTHON_BIN, does NOT checkout a pandas oracle source tree, does NOT set allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.

**Consequence.** In CI as shipped today, all 89 live_oracle_* tests silently pass by skipping. The entire differential conformance layer — the exact thing the README markets as "differential conformance tests verify against the actual pandas oracle" — is a no-op in CI. The only thing CI actually verifies is the 1249 pre-generated packet fixtures (FixtureExpected mode, golden-file comparison), which themselves were generated by some earlier pandas version that is not pinned anywhere (br-frankenpandas-8irq covers the version pin separately).

**Severity.** CRITICAL — the core product claim (pandas parity, verified by oracle) is not actually enforced by CI. Any regression that only the live oracle would catch (e.g. a new pandas point release changing semantics we match; a Rust-side bug that golden fixtures predate) lands on main silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Count-and-fail: HarnessConfig gains a require_live_oracle: bool, default false. When true, OracleUnavailable converts to test failure instead of early-return. ci.yml sets the env var that flips this on.
2. CI installs pandas: ci.yml adds a Python setup step + pip install pandas==PINNED_VERSION (coordinates with br-frankenpandas-8irq). Then clones / mirrors legacy_pandas_code or points oracle_root at the pip-installed pandas via allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.
3. Skip counter: LiveOracleReport aggregates per-test skipped/run/failed counts, printed at end of lib-test run. CI asserts skipped_count == 0 or an explicit allowlist.
4. README update: add "When live pandas is unavailable, these 89 tests skip silently — not a gap in the oracle, a gap in the env" disclosure until (1)-(3) land.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+ ~12 lines), fp-conformance HarnessConfig + tests (+ ~60 lines for the counter + flag). No code-logic change.

Parallel bead: br-frankenpandas-8irq (pin pandas version) becomes a prerequisite for this bead — landing live-oracle-in-CI without a pinned pandas causes CI flakiness on any pandas update.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: DataFrame constructor + merge pub fns lack fuzz targets
description:
/testing-fuzzing coverage audit: DataFrame constructor + merge fuzz targets missing.

**Gap analysis.** Per /testing-fuzzing Rule 3 (every pub fn accepting untrusted input must have a fuzz target). Current coverage:

- fuzz_dataframe_eval — DataFrame.eval (br existing)
- fuzz_pivot_table — pivot_table (br existing)
- fuzz_groupby_sum, fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch — groupby partials (br existing)
- fuzz_join_series — series-level joins (br existing)
- fuzz_dataframe_op_chain — stateful op sequences (br-wr9n)
- fuzz_parallel_dataframe — concurrency (br-bahw)

**Uncovered pub fns (all take untrusted Vec<Scalar> or &DataFrame):**

1. DataFrame::from_dict(column_order, data)                — 14843
2. DataFrame::from_dict_mixed(...)                         — 15121
3. DataFrame::from_dict_with_index(...)                    — 15425
4. DataFrame::from_dict_with_index_mixed(...)              — 15451
5. DataFrame::from_records(records, column_order, labels)  — 15037
6. DataFrame::from_tuples(records, columns)                — 15055
7. DataFrame::from_series(series_list)                     — 14786
8. merge_dataframes(left, right, on, join_type)            — fp-join 1102
9. merge_dataframes_on(left, right, on, join_type)         — fp-join 1092
10. merge_dataframes_on_with(left, right, left_on, right_on, join_type) — fp-join 817

Each is a canonical fuzz Rule 3 target: user-supplied data (potentially crafted) flows in, frame constructed / joined, results must preserve shape invariants.

**Skill rule invoked:** Rule 3 (fuzz every untrusted-input pub fn).

**Severity.** MEDIUM — same severity as the jkhg (expr) and gpxk (SQL) slices already shipped this session. No known bug, but a structural hole in fuzz coverage of the most-used public API.

**Fix shape (four new targets this slice):**

1. fuzz_dataframe_from_dict_bytes — bytes projected into a tiny (name→values) dict; call DataFrame::from_dict; assert ok(df) has row count == inferred n.
2. fuzz_dataframe_from_records_bytes — bytes projected into a rectangular Vec<Vec<Scalar>>; call DataFrame::from_records; assert rows == records.len.
3. fuzz_dataframe_from_series_bytes — bytes projected into Vec<Series> with shared index; call DataFrame::from_series; assert cols == series.len.
4. fuzz_dataframe_merge_bytes — two small frames with a shared 'k' column; call merge_dataframes(..., JoinType::{Inner,Left,Right,Outer}); assert per-join-type row-count bound (inner <= min; left >= left.rows for unique keys; outer >= max).

Each:
- pub fn fuzz_*_bytes in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs with size guard (≤ 64 KB).
- fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_*.rs wrapper.
- [[bin]] entry in fuzz/Cargo.toml.
- fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with 4 seeds.
- fuzz/artifacts/<target>/README.md.
close_reason: Completed merge fuzz invariant hardening and clippy cleanup

title: [HIGH] docs: doctest never run in CI — 8 existing doctests silently unverified + docs.rs will fail at release
description:
Doctest coverage audit.

**Observation.**
- grep for `/// \`\`\`rust` / `/// \`\`\`` / `/// \`\`\`no_run` / `/// \`\`\`ignore` across crates/*/src/lib.rs finds only **8 doctests total** — 2 in frankenpandas, 4 in fp-index, 2 in fp-columnar. The 10 other crates have zero doctests.
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs `cargo test --workspace` at line 32 but does NOT run `cargo test --doc --workspace`. By default `cargo test --workspace` runs `#[test]` fns and integration tests but NOT doctests (those only run with `--doc` or when the default test target is configured to include them).
- Independent confirmation: I grepped for "doc" in ci.yml — returns only the cargo-llvm-cov-related word "Documentation" as false positive, no `--doc` invocation.

**Consequence.**
- The 8 existing doctests never execute in CI. A code example in a public README-level function that compiles today can be silently broken by an API change.
- More importantly, doctests are how Rust libraries document their API contracts with runnable examples. The public-API-contract bead (br-7cfm) called for documenting `# Panics:` on 1530 pub fns; good doctest coverage is the complementary half — runnable examples that VERIFY the documented behavior.
- docs.rs builds doctests when hosting crate docs. Once the release workflow lands (br-4clx) and docs.rs starts serving frankenpandas, any broken doctest fails the docs build → docs.rs shows "docs build failed" instead of the actual API reference → users land on a broken docs page from crates.io.

**Severity.** HIGH — compounds with br-7cfm (panic contract) and br-4clx (release). Without doctest coverage, the public API's "how do I use this?" examples rot silently until a docs.rs build fails publicly at release time.

**Fix shape:**

1. ci.yml adds `cargo test --doc --workspace` as a distinct step after the main `cargo test --workspace`. Catches doctest failures on every PR.
2. Add doctests to the existing pub fn docs systematically:
   - Priority 1: DataFrame constructors (from_dict, new, from_series, from_records) — most-used entry points.
   - Priority 2: Series arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) — high visibility in README examples.
   - Priority 3: GroupBy core (sum, mean, agg_named, rolling) — conformance-heavy surfaces where doctest doubles as conformance sample.
   - Priority 4-N: Remaining public fns by frequency of use (informed by br-zk1j API matrix once that lands).

   Target: lift from 8 doctests to ~150 over several passes. Each passing doctest also serves as a user-facing example, reducing the pure `# Panics:` prose burden from br-7cfm.

3. `cargo test --doc --doc-args="..."`: for slow doctests, use `rust ignore` or `rust no_run` to bail out of execution while keeping the example visible on docs.rs.
4. Doctest regression: add a `scripts/doctest_count.sh` that fails CI if the doctest count shrinks between commits. Prevents silent removal of examples.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+1 step), ~150 lines of doctest additions (parallelizable across agents, one pub fn at a time), 1 small script. No source logic changes.

Related:
- br-7cfm (panic contract) — doctests verify the panic contract in addition to documenting it.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — doctests build on docs.rs at release; adding them now means no surprise at release time.
- br-zk1j (API matrix) — the matrix can mark "has doctest: yes/no" as an extra coverage column.

title: [LOW] license: custom MIT+Rider is non-standard for Rust ecosystem — dual MIT/Apache-2.0 is convention (policy decision)
description:
License structure audit — policy question flagged.

**Observation.** `/LICENSE` is "MIT License (with OpenAI/Anthropic Rider)" — a customized MIT that adds a rider clause. No `LICENSE-MIT` / `LICENSE-APACHE` dual-license setup (the Rust-ecosystem convention used by serde, tokio, rand, clap, anyhow, thiserror, and dozens more).

**Context.** Rust crates dual-license as MIT OR Apache-2.0 because:
- **Apache-2.0 provides an explicit patent grant** (something MIT lacks). Corporate users with legal review processes often need this explicit grant.
- Dual-licensing lets downstream projects pick whichever of the two licenses fits their legal requirements.
- The de-facto convention reads in Cargo.toml as `license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"` and two LICENSE-* files at repo root.

Today:
- `license-file = "LICENSE"` per workspace Cargo.toml.
- The LICENSE file says MIT + a custom rider (restricting use by OpenAI / Anthropic per the filename hint).
- Corporate users whose legal review mandates "use only crates with explicit patent grant" (Apache-2.0 / AGPL-3.0 / etc.) will flag frankenpandas as non-compliant at legal review.
- The rider itself may run afoul of crates.io's SPDX-expression validation if we ever switch from `license-file` to `license = "..."`; crates.io accepts SPDX identifiers, not custom riders.

**Severity.** LOW — it's a policy question, not a technical gap. Severity depends on the adoption audience:
- Pure-OSS / academic audience: LOW, status quo is fine.
- Corporate / Fortune-500 audience: arguably MEDIUM, patent grant matters for legal review.
- Calibrating honestly as LOW because (a) the question needs a human policy decision, and (b) most users of a 0.1.0 library aren't corporate yet.

This is a POLICY-QUESTION bead, not a "add this file" bead — the maintainer needs to decide before any code change lands.

**Fix shape — three options for the maintainer to choose:**

1. **Keep as-is.** Custom MIT + Rider. Document in README why (if the rider is intentional policy against certain AI providers' training-data use, keep it and accept the corporate-review friction).

2. **Dual-license MIT OR Apache-2.0.** Drop the rider (or keep it on MIT only). Add:
   - `LICENSE-MIT` — plain MIT, no rider.
   - `LICENSE-APACHE` — plain Apache-2.0.
   - Cargo.toml `license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"` (SPDX).
   - `license-file` removed.
   - README "License" section: "frankenpandas is dual-licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0. You may pick either license for your use."

3. **Triple-license.** Keep the custom MIT+Rider AS ONE OPTION + add Apache-2.0 + plain MIT:
   - SPDX doesn't support "OR" with non-standard licenses, so this forces `license-file` + a README explainer listing all three options.
   - Most complex; only worth it if the rider is genuinely important AND we want corporate adoption.

**Severity.** LOW (policy question, maintainer decides) — but flagging because it's a release-time decision that will affect every user who ever consumes the library. Deferring past 0.1.0 publish means changing terms in 0.2.0, which is noisy for downstream users who pinned 0.1.x.

Blast radius of option 2: +2 LICENSE files, -1 current LICENSE file if the rider gets dropped entirely (or retained only in the MIT variant). README "License" section update. Cargo.toml workspace.package license switch. Zero source changes.

Prerequisites:
- **HUMAN POLICY DECISION.** An agent cannot and should not pick which license option ships.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — license policy is fixed at first publish; decide before 0.1.0.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release) — license metadata goes to crates.io on first publish.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — contributors must accept whatever license applies; CONTRIBUTING documents the rule.
- README hero section — currently says "MIT + OpenAI/Anthropic rider" per the Shields badge.
close_reason: Maintainer-decision bead. The three license options (MIT+Rider / dual MIT-OR-Apache-2.0 / triple) are fully documented in the bead body. No file changes an agent can safely make here; reopening / re-filing is the right path if the maintainer wants to record a choice in repo rather than here.

[MEDIUM] fp-frame grouped-window code keeps unused agg_name parameter (r4au)
description:
Reviewing 2f80719 (br-frankenpandas-r4au, cod): The new as_index=true path through apply_grouped_rolling and apply_grouped_resample inserts 'let _ = agg_name;' (lines 28026 + 28075 of crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs) to silence the unused-parameter lint after the refactor changed the closure signature. This is a code smell:

- Marking a parameter as 'let _ =' instead of removing it from the function signature signals incomplete refactor and forces every caller to pass a value the body never reads.
- Removes the documentation contract that 'agg_name' was used to identify the aggregation in error messages or evidence ledgers — future maintainers won't know whether to plumb it through or drop it entirely.

Suggested fix: either thread agg_name into the new path (e.g. wrap errors with op_name, or stamp it into the per-group evidence record) or remove it from the apply_grouped_rolling/apply_grouped_resample helpers entirely and update both call sites. Pick one — don't leave _ markers.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: superseded in-tree. Commit ac1550b (br-frankenpandas-ch4o, fix grouped window fallback schema) removed the agg_name parameter from apply_grouped_rolling entirely (and the sibling apply_grouped_resample site) rather than threading it through, which is the second option the bead proposed. grep 'let _ = agg_name' across fp-frame/src/lib.rs is now empty; closure signatures no longer carry the unused parameter. No follow-up action needed.

title: [HIGH] REVIEW: IO live oracle dtype/options parity drifts
description:
Found while building frankenpandas-czmt IO conformance matrix.

1. csv_read_frame with csv_decimal=",", input price\n"1,50"\n"3,75"\n. Expected from live pandas: ["1,50", "3,75"] strings. Actual FrankenPandas: [1.5, 3.75] floats.

2. csv_read_frame with csv_true_values=["1"], csv_false_values=["0"], input flag\n1\n0\n. Expected from live pandas: [1, 0] ints. Actual FrankenPandas: [true, false] bools.

3. csv_read_frame missing-heavy numeric column, input a,b,c\n,NA,NaN\n1,,x\n. Expected from live pandas: column a [null, 1.0]. Actual FrankenPandas: [null, 1].

4. dataframe_to_json_records with duplicate index and column a=[1, null]. Expected live pandas JSON has 1.0 because null promotes numeric column to float. Actual FrankenPandas JSON preserves 1.

5. csv_read_frame with csv_parse_dates=["ts"], input ts,value\n2024-01-15 10:30:00,1\n2024-01-15T10:30:00Z,2\n. Expected from live pandas: second ts remains "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z" under inferred-format parsing. Actual FrankenPandas: "2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00".

Impact: IO read/serialize paths are not matching pandas dtype coercion, parse_dates, and option precedence for these edge cases.

title: [EPIC] SQL backend abstraction — trait + PostgreSQL + MySQL
description:
Pandas-parity SQL IO across multiple backends. Today fp_io::read_sql / write_sql hardcode rusqlite::Connection; pandas read_sql/to_sql works with any SQLAlchemy-compatible engine (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL, Oracle, ...). Multi-slice umbrella; file child beads per slice.

1. pub trait SqlConnection in fp-io (DBAPI2-like surface): execute_query(sql, params) -> RowIter, execute_statement(sql, params) -> usize, with_transaction<F>(f) -> Result<T>. Minimum surface to cover rusqlite, tokio-postgres, and mysql_async concrete backends behind the same trait.

2. Cargo features: sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql. Each feature brings its backend crate and a wrapper impl SqlConnection for that backend's Connection type. Additive; disabling sql-sqlite drops the rusqlite dep.

3. read_sql / write_sql / read_sql_table / read_sql_query signatures move from rusqlite::Connection to <C: SqlConnection>. Existing SQLite call sites keep working because rusqlite::Connection gets the wrapper impl.

4. Param binding: closes frankenpandas-tk3k by letting the public surface accept params: &[&(dyn ToSqlBindable)] routed through each backend's type-specific binder.

5. chunksize streaming: read_sql(.., chunksize=Some(n)) returns impl Iterator<Item=Result<DataFrame, ...>> to match pandas.

6. coerce_float: read_sql(.., coerce_float=true) promotes NUMERIC / DECIMAL / MONEY to Float64 at row-materialization time.

7. Live-oracle conformance: packet fixtures per backend (sqlite/postgres/mysql) verifying round-trip parity with pandas' pd.read_sql(engine) for each.

Blocker for m3e8 closure: the README now honestly says 'SQL: SQLite-only via rusqlite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned' (commit-pending, br-m3e8). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing — at minimum slices (1)-(3).

Decomposition strategy: (1) is the keystone — pick between (a) a minimal DBAPI2-style trait sufficient to host all three backends without leaking backend-specific types into the public API, vs (b) a typestate / erased-impl approach. (2) feature-gates each backend behind Cargo features to keep binary size bounded. (4)-(7) then unblock incrementally behind (1)+(2)+(3).
close_reason: Completed minimum SQL backend abstraction slice: generic SqlConnection APIs are in place, rusqlite is gated behind default sql-sqlite, and fp-io builds without default SQL backend features

title: [HIGH] ci: monolithic 'checks' job lumps fmt/lint/test/conformance/gates — per-PR triage slow + parallelism wasted
description:
CI job-splitting / triage-velocity audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/ci.yml` today has TWO jobs:
  - `checks`: monolithic — runs fmt, cargo check, clippy, workspace test, conformance tests, bench smoke, phase2c gate, governance gate. Each failure surfaces as "checks failed."
  - `fuzz-regression`: new, added recently by br-zjme landing — focused on fuzz corpus regression.

(Note: `checks` also installs Python + pandas as of the br-d6xa fix landing — good.)

**Problem with monolithic checks.**

- Median time for a "checks" run (per recent CI artifacts the ~175s test step + ~60-90s clippy + ~80s install-llvm-cov dead step [br-0a83] + smaller steps) ≈ 5-8 minutes.
- When a single step fails — say, `cargo fmt --check` at step 2 of 15 — every subsequent step is skipped, but the full 5-8 min matrix-slot is consumed because GH Actions doesn't parallelize within a job.
- PR reviewers see ONE red "checks" badge and must expand the logs to find which step failed. If multiple PRs are in flight, triage is serial.
- Retrying a fmt failure re-runs all 15 steps. A minor formatting fix wastes 5-8 min of runner time.
- Caching is per-job. Since `checks` does everything, it sees one cache key for 15 disparate workloads; `clippy` and `test` can't benefit from each other's cache separately.

**How the industry solves it (pattern used by tokio, arrow, polars, serde, etc.):**

```yaml
jobs:
  fmt:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo fmt --check ]

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings ]

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo test --workspace --all-targets ]

  conformance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [test]   # conformance requires a green test first
    steps: [ install-pandas, cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib ]

  gates:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [conformance]
    steps: [ ./scripts/phase2c_gate_check.sh, ./scripts/governance_gate_check.sh ]

  fuzz-regression:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest  (already exists)
```

Splits produce:
1. Five PARALLEL jobs (fmt / lint / test / conformance / gates / fuzz-regression) — runner total time drops from ~7 min serial to ~4 min parallel (the longest job is the bound).
2. Per-job status in the PR UI: reviewers see 5 named badges, spot the red one immediately.
3. Retry cost: fmt-only retry reruns fmt only (20-30s instead of 5-8 min).
4. Per-job cache keys: lint + test caches separately; no cross-contamination.

**Severity.** HIGH — impacts every single PR in perpetuity. With the swarm's current velocity (20+ commits/day), 5-8 min of CI serialization per PR adds up to hours-per-week of wall-clock friction that splits would eliminate. Also: the monolithic job blocks future additions (br-0l5r cross-platform matrix, br-kmbc feature-matrix) because adding them to `checks` would push it to ~15 min; separate jobs let each matrix ship independently.

**Fix shape:**

1. ci.yml refactor:
   - Split `checks` into fmt / lint / test / conformance / gates (~50 line reorg, per above).
   - Fuzz-regression job stays as-is.
   - Add `needs:` dependencies so conformance runs AFTER test (no sense running conformance if basic tests fail).

2. Optional enhancement (can ship same PR or follow-up):
   - Add `strategy.matrix` to test job for OS cross-platform (br-0l5r integrates naturally here).
   - Add `strategy.matrix` to test job for feature combos (br-kmbc).

3. Keep llvm-cov install (br-0a83) in its own coverage job or remove the install line until coverage is actually wired.

4. README / CONTRIBUTING (br-6d5s) gets a brief "CI jobs" section listing each job's responsibility.

Blast radius: ci.yml only (+~50 lines refactor, -~15 lines from the existing checks mega-job). Zero source-logic changes. No new dependencies.

Prerequisites: None. Can land independently.

**Related:**
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — integrates into the split test job.
- br-kmbc (feature matrix) — integrates into the split test job.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-hg60 (secret scan) — natural new jobs alongside the split structure.
- br-zjme (fuzz CI — already landed with its own job) — this bead generalizes that pattern.
- br-0a83 (llvm-cov dead step) — cleaned up during the refactor.

title: fp-conformance: fixture FP-P2C-005 groupby_sum_order expected_series matches insertion-order, not pandas default
description:
Observed: tests::packet_filter_runs_groupby_packet (FP-P2C-005) — 1/4 case fails: groupby_sum_order_strict.

Mismatch: actual=[Utf8("a"), Utf8("b")] (sorted alphabetical, ddof pandas default), expected=[Utf8("b"), Utf8("a")] (insertion order).

pandas' DataFrame.groupby('key').sum() (no explicit sort=False) sorts the result index alphabetically. The fixture's expected_series uses insertion order [b, a] which would only match groupby(sort=False). No 'sort' parameter is encoded in the fixture or the harness for this operation, so the harness defaults to sort=True (pandas default), and fp-frame correctly produces sorted [a, b].

Fix: update fixture expected_series to the pandas-default sorted form: index [a, b], values [6.0, 4.0]. Preserves the sum-correctness check while aligning with the default-sort semantics actually exercised.
close_reason: shipped caa4106: fixture expected_series re-aligned to pandas-default sort (a,b)/(6.0,4.0). Also upgraded bare assert in packet_filter_runs_groupby_packet to include report payload in panic msg. Test now 4/4 cases pass.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: 6 pub SQL-reading fns in fp-io lack fuzz targets — crafted query strings hit driver unsafety undetected
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill decision tree: "Database engine → STATEFUL + GRAMMAR-BASED".

**Observation.** fp-io exposes SQL surfaces that accept query strings:

  read_sql<C: SqlConnection>(conn, query: &str)            → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3302
  read_sql_with_options<C: SqlConnection>(conn, ..., options) → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3309
  read_sql_with_index_col                                  → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3360
  read_sql_table<C: SqlConnection>(conn, table_name: &str) → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3429
  read_sql_table_with_index_col                           → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3375
  read_sql_table_columns                                   → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3448

(Note: The SqlConnection trait already exists — br-frankenpandas-fd90's slice 1 or a parallel agent's work on that epic has landed. Still, the strings that flow to the backend are untrusted user input.)

None of these six pub fns have any fuzz target. Input strings flow directly into rusqlite / (future: tokio-postgres / mysql_async) prepared-statement handling. A crafted query could:
- Panic the driver on an unterminated statement.
- OOM via an expression that allocates an enormous result buffer.
- Bypass our layer's own validation to trigger a backend-side bug.
- Hit the column-name → Scalar::* translator with an unexpected SQLite type (e.g. BLOB, NULL with PRAGMA type_affinity off) and crash the downstream DataFrame builder.

**Skill rule invoked:** Rule 3 (fuzz every untrusted-input pub fn) + the "Database engine" branch of the decision tree. The decision-tree recommendation is STATEFUL + GRAMMAR-BASED: generate random-but-valid SQL, run it against a shadow model (an in-memory SQLite with known state).

**Severity.** HIGH — SQL is the highest-risk IO path we expose. Even with `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` on our side, the backend drivers (rusqlite's C-interop for SQLite, future PG/MySQL libs) are unsafe-rich code. A crafted query that exercises an untested path can trigger backend-level UB; our layer's coverage of that path determines whether we detect it or silently forward the result.

**Fix shape:**

Phase 1 — minimum viable fuzz target (crash detector):
1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sql_read.rs` — wraps `fp_conformance::fuzz_read_sql_bytes`.
2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_read_sql_bytes(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Set up an in-memory rusqlite::Connection with a fixed schema (say, 3 tables: `t1(a INT, b TEXT)`, `t2(k TEXT PRIMARY KEY, v REAL)`, `empty_t()`) populated with 10-20 deterministic rows.
   - Convert data bytes to UTF-8 lossy, clamp to 4096 chars.
   - Call `read_sql(&conn, &query_str)`. Expect Ok or Err; assert no panic.
   - If Ok, assert the returned DataFrame's column count matches the query's SELECT list arity (via a cheap regex on the query string — only for statements that parse as SELECT).

Phase 2 — structure-aware grammar fuzz:
3. Use `SQLsmith` or a small hand-grammar that generates syntactically valid SQL against the fixture schema. Feeds the grammar's output to the same fuzz_read_sql_bytes harness. Catches deeper code paths (JOIN, GROUP BY, subquery, UNION, window functions) that random bytes would never produce.

Phase 3 — STATEFUL:
4. `fuzz_sql_session_bytes` — byte input parsed as a sequence of SQL statements (CREATE, INSERT, UPDATE, SELECT, DELETE). Run each against a rusqlite connection + a shadow BTreeMap model. After each statement, assert the connection's state equals the shadow model's state. Detects state-corruption bugs that single-query fuzz misses.

5. Eventually (once br-fd90 PostgreSQL backend lands): differential fuzz — same grammar-generated SQL against rusqlite AND tokio-postgres, assert same result DataFrame. Catches backend-specific divergence.

Seed corpus (5 minimum for Phase 1):
- empty string
- `SELECT 1` (trivial valid)
- `SELECT * FROM t1` (schema-referencing)
- `SELECT * FROM nonexistent_table` (error path)
- `DROP TABLE t1; SELECT * FROM t1` (multi-statement — likely rejected, assert no panic)

**Related:**
- br-fd90 (SQL backend epic) — slice 7 (live-oracle conformance per backend) naturally overlaps with Phase 2 grammar fuzz.
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — adds new targets to the existing regression gate.
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — pandas's `pd.read_sql(query, engine)` can serve as oracle for Phase 2 fuzz.

title: fp-frame: DataFrame.mode() preserves Int64 when padded with nulls (dtype parity)
description:
Observed failure: fp-conformance tests::packet_filter_runs_dataframe_rank_mode_packet fails on FP-P2D-057/dataframe_mode_ties_strict: 'dataframe column b mismatch at idx=0: actual=Float64(3.0), expected=Int64(3)'.

Fixture: columns a=[1,1,2,2] (2 tied modes) and b=[3,3,3,4] (1 mode). max_modes=2, so column b gets padded with Null(NaN) to match. Expected (per pandas oracle): column b dtype stays Int64, null cell remains Null(NaN) with int64 kind. Actual: fp-frame widens to Float64.

Root: crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:411 fn mode_output_dtype returns DType::Float64 when saw_missing || saw_int is true. The branch 'saw_float || saw_missing' conflates 'original dtype included floats' with 'padding added a null' — the latter shouldn't drive dtype widening because the original column was Int64 and pandas keeps Int64 with a null.

Fix: separate the padding-induced saw_missing from genuine float widening. If we saw only Int64 values plus nulls (from padding), return Some(DType::Int64); only widen to Float64 when genuine Float64 values are present or when Bool+Int mix. Unit test: verify mode_output_dtype([Int64, Null]) -> Int64 and column b output preserves Int64 kind.
close_reason: shipped b544717: mode_axis1 pre-promotes Int->Float when any selected source column is Float64. Companion mode_output_dtype dtype-preservation fix and 3 new tests co-landed in 94fcdca. Verified packet_filter_runs_dataframe_rank_mode_packet now passes; 9/9 df_mode tests green; fp-frame clippy clean.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: dictionaries missing for structured formats + sanitizer flags not configured
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's checklist and DICTIONARIES.md reference.

**Observed state:** fuzz/Cargo.toml has 21 [[bin]] blocks, no sanitizer mentions. No fuzz/dictionaries/ dir. No README documenting which sanitizer each target requires or how to run with ASan+UBSan.

**Missing skill requirements:**
- Rule 5: "ASan without UBSan is half a tool. Always enable both." cargo-fuzz on stable Rust defaults to ASan-only. UBSan needs RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' under nightly or a `.cargo/config.toml` override.
- Rule 6: "Run separate sanitizer campaigns with shared corpora." — no documented MSan campaign for the fp-runtime asupersync path (which has unsafe via the dependency graph).
- Rule 4: "Structure-aware beats random bytes 10:1 on structured formats." fp-io parses CSV, JSON, JSONL, Excel, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC — all structured. The Arbitrary-derive macros in fp-conformance's fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes helpers do partial structure-awareness, but format-specific dictionaries (keywords, delimiters, magic bytes) are absent.

**Fix shape:**
1. fuzz/dictionaries/csv.dict   — quoted-newline, escaped-quote, BOM, comma/pipe/tab delimiters, RFC 4180 edge strings.
2. fuzz/dictionaries/json.dict  — {}, [], "\\u", NaN, Infinity, escape sequences, surrogate pairs.
3. fuzz/dictionaries/excel.dict — XLSX magic bytes PK\x03\x04, zip64 markers, shared-strings-table tokens.
4. fuzz/dictionaries/parquet.dict — PAR1 magic, Thrift type tokens, page-header bytes.
5. fuzz/dictionaries/arrow_ipc.dict — ARROW1, stream end marker, schema flatbuffer tokens.
6. fuzz/README.md documenting: per-target dictionary, required sanitizer, recommended runtime, how to reproduce a crash.
7. .cargo/config.toml or fuzz/.cargo/config.toml setting RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' for the `fuzz` profile (nightly-only; behind a feature flag or comment).

Skill reference: DICTIONARIES.md in testing-fuzzing/references.

Priority MEDIUM — no known bug; this is a force multiplier for future fuzz campaigns (expected 5-50x speedup on the JSON/CSV/Excel surfaces per the skill's stated impact).

[MEDIUM] crates.io: 11 of 12 Cargo.toml carry no description/keywords/categories/repository — release-day discoverability gap
description:
crates.io metadata audit.

**Observation.** Out of 12 Cargo.toml files in the workspace, ZERO carry `keywords`, `categories`, `repository`, `homepage`, `documentation`, or `readme`. Only the umbrella `frankenpandas` crate has a `description` field; the 11 fp-* crates have none.

**Impact at crates.io release (br-4clx prerequisite).**
- `description`: crates.io shows "(no description given)" on search results + crate pages. Users scrolling browse results skip.
- `keywords`: crates.io keyword search (e.g. "dataframe", "pandas", "analytics") misses the crate. Up to 5 keywords per crate; each is a free discoverability channel.
- `categories`: crates.io has a curated category hierarchy (database::implementations, data-structures, science, etc.). Without it, the crate doesn't appear in any category browse.
- `repository`: no "Source" button on the crate page. Users can't jump from crates.io to GitHub.
- `homepage`: separate from repository — for projects with a dedicated website (frankensuite-website-development skill implies one exists).
- `documentation`: docs.rs auto-links via crate name so this is non-blocking, but setting it explicitly lets us point to a custom hosted docs if we ever diverge from docs.rs.
- `readme`: per-crate README pointer. By default it auto-detects but it's the kind of thing that silently breaks after a refactor.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — release-day discoverability gap. Not a correctness issue; not a data-safety issue; not a performance issue. Honest calibration: fixing after release means users who adopted 0.1.0 see outdated metadata until 0.1.1 ships; fixing before release is free.

**Fix shape.**

1. Workspace-level `[workspace.package]` gets a shared baseline:
   ```toml
   [workspace.package]
   edition = "2024"
   version = "0.1.0"
   license-file = "LICENSE"
   repository = "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
   homepage = "https://frankenpandas.dev"          # if the site exists
   documentation = "https://docs.rs/frankenpandas"
   readme = "README.md"
   keywords = ["dataframe", "pandas", "analytics", "rust", "columnar"]
   categories = ["database-implementations", "data-structures", "science"]
   ```

2. Each fp-* Cargo.toml [package] inherits via `.workspace = true`:
   ```toml
   [package]
   name = "fp-frame"
   version.workspace = true
   edition.workspace = true
   repository.workspace = true
   homepage.workspace = true
   documentation.workspace = true
   readme.workspace = true
   keywords.workspace = true
   categories.workspace = true
   license-file.workspace = true
   description = "DataFrame and Series core for the frankenpandas workspace."
   ```

   Per-crate description remains bespoke (each fp-* crate does a different thing; workspace-level description would be generic). Keywords can be workspace-inherited because the discoverability terms overlap across crates.

3. Per-crate descriptions to draft (each ≤ 200 chars per crates.io limit):
   - fp-types: "Core Scalar, DType, NullKind primitives for the frankenpandas workspace — the universal type system behind fp-frame."
   - fp-columnar: "Columnar storage primitives (Column, ValidityMask) for pandas-parity dataframes."
   - fp-index: "Flat and multi-level row index types (Index, MultiIndex) for pandas-parity dataframes."
   - fp-runtime: "Runtime policies, evidence ledgers, and asupersync interop for the frankenpandas execution layer."
   - fp-frame: "DataFrame and Series with pandas-API parity and AACE index alignment."
   - fp-io: "IO layer: CSV, JSON, Parquet, Excel, Feather, Arrow IPC, SQL (SQLite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned)."
   - fp-groupby: "GroupBy engine for fp-frame with dense / arena / hash-map execution paths."
   - fp-join: "Merge and join engine (AG-05 leapfrog triejoin) for fp-frame DataFrames."
   - fp-expr: "Expression eval / query parser for fp-frame DataFrames (pandas df.eval/df.query parity)."
   - fp-conformance: "Conformance harness: differential test suite against live pandas oracle."
   - fp-frankentui: "FrankenTUI surface for interactive fp-frame workflows."

4. Categories per crate: mostly shared ("data-structures", "science") but IO crate can claim "filesystem" / "database-implementations"; runtime crate can claim "concurrency"; groupby/join can claim "algorithms".

Blast radius: workspace Cargo.toml + 11 per-crate Cargo.toml edits (~5-10 line additions each). Zero source changes. Per-crate descriptions need proofreading (~1 min each).

Prerequisites:
- br-4clx (release workflow) — this metadata is discovered at publish time; landing this bead BEFORE 4clx's first cargo publish saves a 0.1.1 patch.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release + workspace version inheritance) — same commit wave; natural pairing.
- br-60du (CHANGELOG stale) — another release-day metadata bead.
- br-wskz (docs.rs metadata) — companion: wskz controls docs.rs build config; this bead controls crates.io page content.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — repository URL in Cargo.toml points at where SECURITY.md lives.

title: [HIGH] security: no secret scanning in CI or pre-commit — leaked credentials in commits become permanent git history
description:
Secret / credential hygiene audit.

**Observation.**
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml`: no gitleaks, no truffleHog, no detect-secrets, no similar scanner.
- Repo has ONE pre-commit hook: `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/50-agent-mail.py` (coordination for the multi-agent swarm, not a secret scan).
- No `.pre-commit-config.yaml` (the pre-commit.com framework) — so no ecosystem-standard secret hooks either.
- No `.gitallowed` / `.gitleaksignore` / `.git-crypt` / `.gitattributes` with secret-relevant entries — nothing that suggests prior secret-scan discipline.

**Context.** This is a public-library repo that will ship to crates.io (once br-4clx lands). The multi-agent swarm produces 20+ commits/day from multiple authors. Real surface areas where secrets leak:
- CI artifacts: a test or fixture that inadvertently embeds a token (env var leaked into a string literal).
- Examples in rustdoc (doctests) pulling from real services with hardcoded API keys for demo purposes.
- Oracle script `oracle/pandas_oracle.py`: if it ever calls a remote service (it currently doesn't, but nothing prevents a PR adding one with a key).
- Sidecar config in `artifacts/`: JSON reports with paths, usernames, or host identifiers that constitute PII/infra info.

**Severity.** HIGH — git history is append-only for practical purposes. A secret committed today is discoverable by any scraper forever. Cleanup requires force-pushes (destructive, see skill rules about confirmations) plus rotation of every leaked credential.

**Fix shape:**

1. CI job `security`: runs gitleaks on every PR and on the `main` branch tip. Fails CI on any detection.
   ```yaml
   - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
     env:
       GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   ```
   Alternatively `trufflehog` for broader pattern coverage. Either is one step (~15s runtime).

2. Pre-commit hook `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/20-secret-scan.sh`:
   - Runs `gitleaks protect --staged` on every commit.
   - Exits non-zero on finding.
   - Extends the existing 50-agent-mail.py single-hook pattern. Use numeric prefix ordering so secret scan runs before agent-mail (fast-fail on credentials before coordinating the commit with other agents).

3. `.gitleaks.toml` config:
   - Allowlist obviously-non-secret patterns (fixture data, example API URLs, placeholder strings).
   - Tighten rulesets for AWS / GCP / GitHub / npm / Docker Hub / Slack webhook patterns.

4. Documentation in CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s): "Never commit real credentials. Use env vars for CI secrets via GitHub Actions secrets. All test fixtures must use placeholder tokens starting with `test_` or `fake_`."

5. One-time retroactive scan: run gitleaks against the entire repo history NOW (before the gate goes live) so any prior leaks can be rotated.

Blast radius: 1 new CI job, 1 new pre-commit hook, 1 new .gitleaks.toml, 1 section in CONTRIBUTING.md. Zero source code changes beyond any secrets that the retroactive scan surfaces.

Related:
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — disclosure policy for external reports; this bead prevents us leaking secrets of our own.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — where the documented policy lives.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) — detects vulns in deps; this bead detects secrets in our own commits.

title: [MEDIUM] concurrency: Index uses std::cell::OnceCell (!Sync); blocks Arc<DataFrame> sharing + future rayon work
description:
Deadlock / concurrency audit finding (post-/deadlock-finder-and-fixer skill).

**Audit scope.** Scanned all fp-* crates for Mutex / RwLock / OnceCell / OnceLock / Arc / thread::spawn / rayon / tokio / AtomicX usage. Surface area is extremely small:

- `fp-runtime/src/asupersync/transport.rs`: `Arc<Mutex<BTreeMap>>` for the InMemoryTransport backend.
- `fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:22655`: test-helper `phase2c_artifact_test_lock` gating artifact-writing tests.
- `fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:23911`: test-internal `Arc<Mutex<Vec<String>>>` lifecycle-hook recorder.

**Deadlock analysis (no findings).**
- InMemoryTransport: single mutex, always acquired alone, released before return. No AB-BA possible.
- phase2c_artifact_test_lock: correctly recovers from poisoning via `into_inner()` (avoids 18-test cascade failure).
- LifecycleHooks test: single-threaded test driver, `.lock().unwrap()` acceptable scope.
- OnceCell init closures on `Index` (`detect_duplicates` / `detect_sort_order`) are pure functions over owned data; no re-entry path.

**Legitimate finding: Sync contract gap on Index / DataFrame.**

`crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:3` imports `std::cell::OnceCell` (not `std::sync::OnceLock`). `OnceCell` is `!Sync`, so:

```
duplicate_cache: OnceCell<bool>,       // line 193 — !Sync
sort_order_cache: OnceCell<SortOrder>, // line 196 — !Sync
```

makes `Index` itself `!Sync`. `DataFrame` inherits `!Sync` through its `Index` field. Users cannot wrap `Arc<DataFrame>` to share across threads — they must clone per-thread.

This is documented in `fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes` (br-bahw) which uses clone-per-thread as the workaround. DISC-009 candidate.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — not a bug today (single-threaded execution is the README's documented stance), but a hard ceiling on future parallelism work. Any future rayon-integration bead (listed in Roadmap "Parallel execution (rayon)") has this as a prerequisite.

**Fix shape.**

1. `crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:3`: `use std::cell::OnceCell;` → `use std::sync::OnceLock;`
2. Field declarations: `OnceCell<bool>` → `OnceLock<bool>` and same for `SortOrder`.
3. `OnceCell::new()` → `OnceLock::new()` in constructors.
4. `get_or_init` API is identical across the two types (both accept `FnOnce() -> T`). Zero call-site changes.
5. Cost: `OnceLock` uses atomic ops internally; one-time cost on first access. Read-hot path (`.get()` on initialized) is lock-free; same as `OnceCell` read-hot path.
6. Benefit: `Index` + `DataFrame` + all downstream types become `Sync`. Unblocks `Arc<DataFrame>` shared reads across threads.

Blast radius:
- 1 use statement + 4 field-type changes + 4 constructor-default changes in fp-index.
- Zero changes in fp-frame or downstream (they only touch these fields through `Index`'s methods).
- Remove the "DataFrame is Send but NOT Sync" caveat from `fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes` rustdoc + enable `Arc<DataFrame>` sharing in the harness (optional follow-up).

**Related.**
- br-bahw (closed): parallel-dataframe fuzz target currently uses clone-per-thread. After this fix, the harness can Arc-share.
- Roadmap "Parallel execution (rayon)" row: blocks on this.
- Skill rule: strongest oracle available → Sync contract is the precondition.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no corpus minimization cadence (cargo fuzz cmin / tmin) — Rule 8 violation; corpora will bloat once zjme ships
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill Rule 8: "A corpus with 100K entries and the same coverage as 500 entries is 200x slower, not 200x better. Corpus minimization is not optional — it is a correctness requirement for efficient fuzzing."

**Observation.** grep for `cargo fuzz cmin`, `cargo fuzz tmin`, `afl-cmin`, or any "corpus.*minim" text across scripts/, .github/, AGENTS.md, and fuzz/ returns ZERO hits. No documented cadence. No scheduled CI job. No script that wraps the minimization.

Separately confirmed: `fuzz/corpus/` doesn't exist (per br-zjme findings earlier in this review session) so today the point is moot — there's nothing to minimize yet. But once br-zjme ships persistent corpora, the minimization discipline needs to be in place, otherwise corpora grow unboundedly and fuzz exec/s collapses.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Rule 8: minimize-corpus is a correctness requirement.
- Rule 9: "Minimize BEFORE debugging. Always. cargo fuzz tmin / afl-tmin first." — applies to crash artifacts. A 4KB crash input is unreadable; a 23-byte input often makes the bug obvious.
- Checklist: "Minimize weekly (CRITICAL — bloated corpus wastes cycles)."

**Consequence once corpora exist.** Weekly fuzz runs accumulate corpora:
- libFuzzer's `-merge=1` deduplicates by coverage bitmap at each run (good) but doesn't minimize INPUT SIZE.
- Over weeks the corpus grows from 500 → 50K entries. Individual exec/s remains stable but TOTAL coverage-per-wall-clock degrades because the fuzzer spends time replaying high-coverage paths.
- Crash triage (Rule 9): without `cargo fuzz tmin`, each crash artifact is the byte-for-byte input that tripped libFuzzer. These are typically 100-4000 bytes. Minimizing to the smallest reproducer (5-50 bytes) is what makes the bug obvious on inspection.

**Severity.** HIGH — once the fuzz CI (br-zjme) lands, this bead is the force multiplier that keeps it efficient. Without minimization, zjme ships a slowly-degrading system.

**Fix shape:**

1. `scripts/fuzz_minimize.sh` — runs per-target:
   ```bash
   for target in $(ls fuzz/fuzz_targets/); do
     target=${target%.rs}
     # Minimize each crash artifact down
     for crash in fuzz/artifacts/$target/crash-*; do
       cargo +nightly fuzz tmin "$target" "$crash"
     done
     # Minimize corpus to shrink file count
     cargo +nightly fuzz cmin "$target"
   done
   ```
   Runs weekly via a scheduled CI workflow. Commits the result back via an automated PR.

2. Crash triage pipeline:
   - New pre-PR hook: when a PR adds a fuzz artifact under fuzz/artifacts/, CI runs `cargo fuzz tmin` on it automatically and commits the minimized form.
   - PR reviewers see the minimized artifact; the original is preserved under `fuzz/artifacts/originals/` for forensics.

3. AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md section documenting the flow:
   - "Found a crash? cargo fuzz tmin first. Then cargo fuzz run one more time against the minimized input to confirm reproducibility. Then commit the minimized input as a regression seed under fuzz/corpus/<target>/regression_<short_hash>."
   - See br-pwn9 sibling (crash-to-regression-test automation).

4. CI budget: tmin + cmin run under rch/remote runners, not the PR gate. Weekly cadence is 20-40 min; PR gate adds only the tmin on new artifacts (~30 s per crash).

Blast radius: 1 new script (~30 lines), 1 new scheduled workflow (~40 lines), 1 new pre-PR hook step, 1 CONTRIBUTING.md paragraph. Zero source changes.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI + persistent corpus) — prerequisite. This bead lands on top once corpora exist.
- br-auys (memory/time caps) — tmin on a hang-until-killed input needs a timeout guard to be useful.
- br-gztr (sanitizer config) — tmin + cmin work on sanitizer campaigns too; all campaigns share the corpus per skill Rule 6.

title: fp-conformance: phase2c_artifact_test_lock poison-tolerance (18 test cascade)
description:
Observed: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib reports 18 FAILED / 334 passed with every failure message 'phase2c artifact test lock poisoned: PoisonError { .. }'.

Root: crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:20181 phase2c_artifact_test_lock uses std::sync::Mutex and expect() on a poisoned lock. Any single test panic while holding the lock cascades into failures for every subsequent test that uses it. The lock guards only the side-effect sequencing of artifact-writing tests; the inner tuple is (), so taking a poisoned guard and proceeding is safe.

Fix: replace .expect() with .unwrap_or_else(|err| err.into_inner()) to recover the guard through the poison. Downstream call sites don't need changes. 
Tests: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib should go from 334/18 → 352/0 (or reveal the underlying panic buried by the cascade).
close_reason: shipped 2c98ff2: phase2c_artifact_test_lock now recovers poisoned guards via into_inner() (safe because inner is ()). fp-conformance: 334/18 → 338/14; 4 cascade victims recovered, 14 remaining are distinct root-cause panics (follow-up beads filed separately).

title: [HIGH] ci: no performance regression gate — 'cargo bench' is a no-op, perf_baselines.rs is #[ignore]'d
description:
Applying performance-regression-gate audit to CI + bench infrastructure.

**Observation.**
- ci.yml line 46 runs `cargo bench`. Workspace has NO `[[bench]]` targets in any Cargo.toml (grep for `\[\[bench\]\]` or `criterion` returns empty). So `cargo bench` is currently a no-op smoke test that always passes.
- crates/fp-conformance/tests/perf_baselines.rs (387 lines, March 2026 mtime) exists and IS a perf measurement harness — measures p50/p95/p99 latencies across join / filter workloads.
- BUT: perf_baselines.rs tests are marked `#[ignore]` by default (see the rustdoc header comment). CI does not pass `--include-ignored`. So perf_baselines never runs in CI either.
- Even when run, perf_baselines only `eprintln!` the results. There are zero `assert!(p95 < Duration::from_millis(X))` calls. It's a human-readable benchmark, not a regression gate.
- No stored baseline file (no `artifacts/perf/baseline.json` or similar). Diffing against prior runs is impossible.

**Consequence.** A 10x slowdown in any hot path lands silently. README markets "Profile-proven performance" + claims 87% groupby speedup via memoization (AG-04/AG-05). Nothing enforces that the speedup persists. A future refactor that regresses the groupby O(1) fast path to O(n) would not fail CI — only a hand-run of `perf_baselines` with human comparison would catch it.

**Severity.** HIGH — core product claim is unguarded against regression. Performance claims that aren't measured in CI become theater.

**Fix shape:**
1. Convert perf_baselines.rs to assertion form: each benchmark gains `assert!(p95 < SOME_BUDGET)`. Budgets live in an `assets/perf_budgets.json` committed to the repo. Exceeding budget = fail CI.
2. Remove `#[ignore]` from perf_baselines.rs so tests run in `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`. Scope run to fp-conformance only (`cargo test -p fp-conformance --test perf_baselines -- --nocapture`).
3. OR (preferred): add a real `[[bench]]` block using `criterion` crate. Criterion has built-in regression detection via `-- --save-baseline` / `--baseline`. CI:
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --save-baseline pr
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --baseline main --threshold 5%
     step: fail on > 5% regression
4. Artifact the numbers in CI (upload criterion `target/criterion/**/estimates.json`) so trending is visible over time.
5. Remove the stub `cargo bench` line from ci.yml (currently misleading — it appears to run a benchmark gate but doesn't).

Blast radius: perf_baselines.rs (+30 assertions), assets/perf_budgets.json (+1 file), ci.yml (replace line 46). If going with criterion: crates/fp-conformance/benches/perf.rs (+~200 lines), Cargo.toml (+criterion dep).

Budgets need a first calibration run to capture current numbers before the gate goes live. Expect 1-2 iterations of "CI failed because my laptop-captured baseline differs from the Linux runner" — document the calibration workflow.
close_reason: Replaced no-op cargo bench with a real budgeted perf regression gate and committed perf budgets

[MEDIUM] cod commits bundle co-landed work and label is_misleading (p6vu, x5oy)
description:
Reviewing recent cod commits via git log --since=6h. Pattern observed:

- 94fcdca title: 'br-frankenpandas-p6vu: add grouped resample count conformance test'. Actual diff: 183 lines including the mode_output_dtype Int64-padding fix (which was claimed by br-frankenpandas-gwa2/cc). The grouped-resample-count test is in there but the bulk of the diff is the unrelated gwa2 fix accidentally bundled in.
- 1c9467a title: 'br-frankenpandas-x5oy: add grouped rolling conformance coverage'. Actual diff: 122 lines of fp-frame tests; ok scope-wise but co-mingled with another agent's working tree at the time.

Impact: git blame becomes misleading — bisect for a regression in mode_output_dtype lands on a commit titled 'add grouped resample count' rather than the bead that actually authored the change. Also obscures bead ownership: br-gwa2 close cited 'co-landed in 94fcdca' which is itself titled for a different bead.

Suggested process improvement (not code fix): cod and cc should run 'git diff --stat' before commit and detect off-bead changes; either split into two commits or cite the co-landing pair in the message. Track this as a workflow note for the swarm doctrine.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: accepted as workflow doctrine. The bead body IS the record — commits 94fcdca + 1c9467a bundled co-landed changes under off-bead titles, and the correction (run 'git diff --stat' pre-commit; split or cite co-landed beads in the message) is now captured in the bead log. No code fix. Future cc/cod agents should follow the recommendation at commit time; the swarm doctrine will absorb it via this closed bead.
